walkthrough.title: Hand Out Answer Pads and Pencils
walkthrough.text: Let's get Scattergories set up. Give each player an answer pad and a pencil. Each answer pad has numbered lines from one through twelve, matching the twelve categories you will be filling in each round.

walkthrough.title: Select a Category List
walkthrough.text: Now pick a category list card and place it in the folder or holder so everyone can see all twelve categories. The categories might be things like a boy's name, something cold, or a vacation destination. Make sure the list is visible to every player at the table.

walkthrough.title: Place the Timer and Die
walkthrough.text: Set the three-minute sand timer in the center of the table where everyone can see it. Place the twenty-sided letter die within easy reach. The timer keeps things exciting and the die determines which letter everyone must use.

walkthrough.title: Arrange Seating for Privacy
walkthrough.text: Make sure players have enough space to write without others seeing their answers. The fun of Scattergories comes from the surprise of hearing everyone's answers during scoring. Keep those answer pads hidden until time is up. Ready to play? Let's learn the rules.

summary.title: Hand Out Answer Pads and Pencils
bullets: 1 answer pad + 1 pencil per player
Pad has lines 1-12 matching categories

summary.title: Select a Category List
bullets: Place 1 category list card in holder
All 12 categories must be visible to all players

summary.title: Place the Timer and Die
bullets: 3-minute sand timer in center of table
20-sided letter die within reach

summary.title: Arrange Seating for Privacy
bullets: Players need space to write privately
Keep answers hidden until time expires

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Each round of Scattergories has three phases. First, someone rolls the letter die and announces the letter. Second, everyone writes answers simultaneously for three minutes. Third, players compare answers and score points. A standard game is three rounds with different category lists and letters.

walkthrough.title: Rolling the Letter
walkthrough.text: One player rolls the twenty-sided letter die. The letter that comes up is the required starting letter for all answers this round. Call it out clearly so everyone hears it. If the same letter comes up that you used in a previous round, re-roll.

walkthrough.title: Writing Answers
walkthrough.text: Flip the timer and start writing. For each of the twelve categories, write one answer that starts with the rolled letter. You have three minutes. Work quickly, and if you get stuck on a category for more than a few seconds, skip it and come back. When the timer runs out, all pencils go down immediately.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Your Answers
walkthrough.text: Go through the categories one at a time. Each player reads their answer aloud. You score one point for each unique answer that no other player also wrote. If two or more players wrote the same answer for the same category, none of them score for that entry. Blank entries score zero.

walkthrough.title: Alliterative Bonus
walkthrough.text: Here is a fun twist. If your answer has multiple words that each start with the rolled letter, you score one point per matching word instead of just one point. For example, if the letter is S and you write San Salvador for a city, that is two points because both words start with S.

walkthrough.title: Challenging Answers
walkthrough.text: Any player may challenge an answer they think is invalid or does not fit the category. When someone challenges, the group votes. Majority rules. If the answer is rejected, it scores zero. Challenges keep the game honest and are part of the fun.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: After three rounds, each with a different category list and letter, add up everyone's points from all rounds. The player with the highest total wins. If there is a tie, you can play a sudden-death round with a single category, or just share the victory.

summary.title: Round Structure
bullets: Roll letter → write answers (3 min) → compare and score
Standard game = 3 rounds, different lists and letters each round

Skip and return to hard categories

Unique answer = 1 point; duplicate answer = 0 for all who wrote it
Blank = 0 points

summary.title: Alliterative Bonus
bullets: Multi-word answers where each word starts with the letter = 1 pt per word
Example: letter S, "San Salvador" = 2 pts (if unique)

summary.title: Challenging Answers
bullets: Any player can challenge any answer
Group votes — majority decides
Rejected answer = 0 points

summary.title: Winning the Game
bullets: Sum points from all 3 rounds
Highest total wins
Tie: sudden-death round or shared victory

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Practice
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first round of Scattergories! We will walk through a practice round with four players. Everyone has their answer pad ready and the category list is visible. Let's roll the die and see what letter we get.

walkthrough.title: Roll the Letter
walkthrough.text: Let's say the die lands on the letter B. Call it out. Everyone now needs to write one answer per category where the answer starts with B. Flip the timer and let's go.

walkthrough.title: Writing Phase Begins
walkthrough.text: The timer is running. Player one quickly writes Boston for a vacation destination and banana for a fruit. Player two writes Brazil and blueberry. Player three fills in Barcelona and blackberry. Player four is thinking carefully about each answer.

walkthrough.title: Player Four's Mistake: Overthinking
walkthrough.text: Player four spent two full minutes trying to think of the most creative answer for the first category. They came up with a brilliant, obscure answer, but now they only have one minute left and eight categories are still blank. They rush through the rest but only fill in four more categories. Six blanks means six guaranteed zeros.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Speed Over Perfection
walkthrough.text: The biggest mistake in Scattergories is spending too long on one category. A basic answer that scores one point is infinitely better than a blank that scores zero. Make a quick pass through all twelve categories first. Write the first thing that comes to mind. Then go back and try to upgrade obvious answers to creative ones.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Time
walkthrough.text: Time is up, pencils down. Let's go through the categories. For the vacation destination category, Player one wrote Boston, Player two wrote Brazil, Player three wrote Barcelona, and Player four wrote Bermuda. All different! Everyone scores one point.

walkthrough.title: Player One's Mistake: Writing the Obvious
walkthrough.text: For the fruit category, Player one wrote banana. Player two also wrote banana. Because two players have the same answer, neither of them scores. Player three wrote blackberry, which is unique and scores one point. Player four left it blank. The most obvious answer is the one most likely to be duplicated.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Think Uniquely
walkthrough.text: The first answer that pops into your head is usually the same one that pops into everyone else's head. After your quick first pass, go back and look for answers that are correct but less obvious. For the letter B and a fruit, blueberry or boysenberry is safer than banana because fewer people will write it.

walkthrough.title: The Alliterative Bonus
walkthrough.text: For the celebrity category, Player three wrote Billy Bob Thornton. Both Billy and Bob start with B. That is two bonus-eligible words, so Player three scores two points for that one answer instead of one. Always look for multi-word answers where each word starts with the rolled letter.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's Mistake: Not Challenging
walkthrough.text: For the animal category, Player four wrote Bald Eagle. Player two thinks this does not count because Bald Eagle is two words and Eagle does not start with B. But Player two stays quiet and lets it go. The answer stands and Player four gets a point. Player two should have challenged because the group might have rejected it.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Use Challenges Wisely
walkthrough.text: Challenges are free and part of the game. If an answer seems questionable, speak up. The worst that happens is the group votes to keep it. But if they reject it, you just removed a competitor's point. Don't be aggressive about every answer, but don't be passive either.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Great round! You have seen the three most common mistakes in Scattergories. First, overthinking and leaving categories blank. Second, writing the obvious answer that everyone else also writes. Third, not challenging questionable answers. Keep these lessons in mind and you will score higher every round.

walkthrough.title: Next Steps
walkthrough.text:
Before your next game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategies on balancing speed and creativity.

Second, browse the Appendix for a quick reference on scoring rules and common edge cases.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: The Two-Pass Method
walkthrough.text: The most effective approach in Scattergories is the two-pass method. On your first pass, write the first answer that comes to mind for every category as fast as possible. Do not think, just write. On your second pass, go back and look at your answers. Replace any that feel too obvious with something more creative. This ensures you never have blanks while still hunting for unique answers.

walkthrough.title: Hunt for Alliterative Answers
walkthrough.text: The alliterative bonus is the easiest way to score extra points. For every category, ask yourself if there is a multi-word answer where each word starts with the rolled letter. Names are goldmines for this. Big Bird, Charlie Chaplin, Marilyn Monroe. Places work too. San Salvador, Boca Bay. One two-point answer is worth two basic answers.

walkthrough.title: Avoid the Most Common Answers
walkthrough.text: Think about what the other players are likely writing. For the letter S and a fruit, strawberry is the first thing everyone thinks of. But starfruit or Sumo orange is less obvious. You do not need obscure answers, just ones that are slightly off the beaten path. The goal is to be correct and unique, not clever and wrong.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Challenge
walkthrough.text: Challenges are strategic, not personal. Challenge answers that clearly do not fit the category or where the starting letter is debatable. Do not challenge everything or you will slow the game down and annoy other players. Save challenges for answers that matter, especially if you and another player are close in score.

walkthrough.title: Category-Specific Tricks
walkthrough.text: Some categories lend themselves to creative answers. For person names, think of full names with alliterative potential. For something you find in a specific location, think of unusual but defensible items. For adjectives, remember that many words can be stretched to fit, but keep them defensible in case of a challenge.

walkthrough.title: Playing with Different Group Sizes
walkthrough.text: In a larger group, duplicates are much more common, so creativity matters even more. In a two-player game, duplicates are rare, so speed and filling every category is the priority. Adjust your strategy based on how many players are at the table. More players means you need more unusual answers to stay unique.

summary.title: The Two-Pass Method
bullets: First pass: write first answer for every category, no thinking
Second pass: replace obvious answers with creative ones
Guarantees no blanks while still hunting for uniqueness

summary.title: Hunt for Alliterative Answers
bullets: Multi-word answers with each word matching the letter = bonus pts
Names and places are best: Big Bird, San Salvador
1 two-point answer = 2 basic answers

bullets: Think about what others are writing — avoid it
Slightly off-the-beaten-path > wildly obscure
Goal: correct and unique, not clever and wrong

summary.title: Know When to Challenge
bullets: Challenge clear mismatches, not every borderline answer
Save challenges for when scores are close
Don't slow the game with excessive challenges

summary.title: Playing with Different Group Sizes
bullets: More players = more duplicates → need more creativity
2 players = fewer duplicates → speed and filling > creativity
Adjust strategy based on group size

walkthrough.title: Predict and Avoid
walkthrough.text: The highest-level Scattergories strategy is predicting what other players will write and deliberately avoiding those answers. Think about the most obvious answer for each category, then write something different. If the letter is C and the category is a color, everyone will write crimson or coral. Write celadon or chartreuse instead. This takes practice but dramatically increases your unique answer rate.

walkthrough.title: Category List Familiarity
walkthrough.text: If you play Scattergories regularly, you start to see the same category types over and over. Person names, places, foods, animals, and household items appear on almost every list. Build mental banks of unusual answers for these common categories. When a familiar category appears, you can immediately write a prepared unusual answer instead of defaulting to the obvious.

walkthrough.title: Letter Strengths and Weaknesses
walkthrough.text: Some letters are much harder than others. Letters like S, C, and B have tons of options, so creativity matters most. Letters like K, J, or W have fewer options, so speed matters because everyone struggles. Adjust your strategy based on the letter. For easy letters, go creative. For hard letters, fill categories fast because everyone else will also have blanks.

walkthrough.title: The Defensive Write
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the smartest play is writing the same answer as another player on purpose for a category where you cannot think of anything good. If you suspect a specific player has a strong unique answer, you can write the most obvious answer to potentially cancel out another player's obvious answer, keeping the scores close. This is most useful in tight two or three-player games.

walkthrough.title: Time Banking
walkthrough.text: Instead of spending equal time on all twelve categories, bank your time by speed-writing the easy ones in thirty seconds, then invest the remaining time on the three or four hardest categories. Those hard categories are where most players leave blanks, so any answer at all gives you a scoring advantage.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Psychology
walkthrough.text: During the scoring phase, how you read your answers can influence challenges. Read confidently and move quickly. If you hesitate or sound uncertain, other players are more likely to challenge. Conversely, if another player reads an answer they seem unsure about, that is a good time to challenge. The social dynamics of scoring are part of the game.

summary.title: Predict and Avoid
bullets: Think of the most obvious answer → write something else
Predict what others will write and dodge duplicates
Dramatically increases unique answer rate

summary.title: Category List Familiarity
bullets: Same category types repeat: names, places, foods, animals
Build mental banks of unusual answers for common types
Prepared answer > default obvious answer

summary.title: Letter Strengths and Weaknesses
bullets: Easy letters (S, C, B): creativity matters most
Hard letters (K, J, W): speed matters, fill categories fast
Adjust strategy per letter difficulty

summary.title: The Defensive Write
bullets: Write obvious answer to cancel out another player's obvious answer
Useful in tight 2-3 player games
Sacrifices your point to deny theirs

summary.title: Time Banking
bullets: Speed-write easy categories in 30 sec

entries.definition: A card listing twelve categories that players must fill in during a round. Multiple list cards are included for variety across rounds.
A twenty-sided die with letters of the alphabet. Some difficult letters like Q, U, V, X, Y, and Z are excluded in most editions.
A three-minute timer that limits the writing phase of each round. When the sand runs out, all players must stop writing immediately.
A pad of paper with numbered lines one through twelve for writing answers. Each player uses one sheet per round.
An answer that no other player wrote for the same category. Unique answers score one point. The goal is to write answers that are valid but uncommon.
When two or more players write the same answer for the same category. All players with the duplicate score zero for that category. Case insensitive.
Multi-word answers where each word begins with the rolled letter score one point per matching word instead of the standard one point. The first word must start with the rolled letter as a baseline.
Any player may question the validity of another player's answer. The group votes by majority to accept or reject the challenged answer. Rejected answers score zero.
One complete cycle of rolling the letter die, writing answers for three minutes, and scoring. A standard game consists of three rounds with different category lists and letters.
Names of specific people, places, brands, and other named entities. Proper nouns are valid answers in Scattergories as long as they fit the category.
The letter rolled on the die that all answers must begin with for that round. An answer that does not start with the required letter is invalid.
If the letter die shows the same letter used in a previous round, roll again until a new letter appears.
